title: The Maranoa Drovers
content:
آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ  (Air: ï؟½Little Sally Waters.ï؟½)


آ آ آ آ The night is dark and stormy, and the sky is clouded oï؟½er;
آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ Our horses we will mount and ride away,
آ آ آ آ To watch the squattersï؟½ cattle through the darkness of the night,
آ آ آ آ And weï؟½ll keep them on the camp till break of day.

آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ Chorus

آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ  For weï؟½re going, going, going to Gunnedah so far,
آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ  And weï؟½ll soon be into sunny New South Wales;
آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ  We shall bid farewell to Queensland, with its swampy coolibahï؟½
آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ  Happy drovers from the sandy Maranoa.


آ آ آ آ When the fires are burning bright through the darkness of the night,
آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ And the cattle camping quiet, well, Iï؟½m sure
آ آ آ آ That I wish for two oï؟½clock when I call the other watchï؟½
آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ This is droving from the sandy Maranoa.

آ آ آ آ Our beds made on the ground, we are sleeping all so sound
آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ When weï؟½re wakened by the distant thunderï؟½s roar,
آ آ آ آ And the lightningï؟½s vivid flash, followed by an awful crash-
آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ Itï؟½s rough on drovers from the sandy Maranoa.

آ آ آ آ We are up at break of day, and weï؟½re all soon on the way,
آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ For we always have to go ten miles or more;
آ آ آ آ It donï؟½t do to loaf about, or the squatter will come outï؟½
آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ Heï؟½s strict on drovers from the sandy Maranoa.

آ آ آ آ We shall soon be on the Moonie, and weï؟½ll cross the Barwon, too;
آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ Then weï؟½ll be out upon the rolling plains once more;
آ آ آ آ Weï؟½ll shout ï؟½Hurrah! for old Queensland, with its swampy coolibah,
آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ And the cattle that come off the Maranoa.ï؟½
meaning:
This evocative piece by Banjo Paterson (Andrew Barton) represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Banjo Paterson (Andrew Barton)'s bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
